section.title: A Call to Rebuild the Temple
section.sectionRationale: The first dated oracle of the book, delivered on the first day of the sixth month in the second year of Darius, 520 BC. It is one complete unit: the LORD's rebuke of a people who panel their own houses while His house lies in ruins, His call to consider their ways and build, and the remnant's swift obedience under Zerubbabel and Joshua.
section.thesis: When the returned exiles put their own comfort before the house of God, the LORD confronts their misplaced priorities, calls them to consider their ways and build, and then stirs their hearts with the promise, I am with you.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the rebuke for a temple left in ruins; the call to consider their ways and rebuild for God's glory; and the remnant's obedience as the LORD stirs their spirit.

units.label: The rebuke: a house in ruins
units.note: these people say the time has not come

verses.text: In the second year of the reign of Darius, on the first day of the sixth month, the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet to Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, stating
that this is what the {{lordOfHosts|LORD of Hosts}} says: “These people say, ‘The time has not yet come to rebuild the house of the LORD.’”
Then the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet, saying:
“Is it a time for you yourselves to live in your paneled houses, while this house lies in {{ruins|ruins}}?”

units.label: Consider your ways and build
units.note: you have planted much but harvested little

verses.text: Now this is what the LORD of Hosts says: “{{consider|Consider carefully your ways}}.

You have planted much but harvested little. You eat but never have enough. You drink but never have your fill. You put on clothes but never get warm. You earn wages to put into a bag pierced through.”
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Consider carefully your ways.
Go up into the hills, bring down lumber, and build the house, so that I may take pleasure in it and be {{glorified|glorified}}, says the LORD.

You expected much, but behold, it amounted to little. And what you brought home, I blew away. Why? declares the LORD of Hosts. Because My house still lies in ruins, while each of you is busy with his own house.
Therefore, on account of you the heavens have withheld their dew and the earth has withheld its crops.
I have summoned a drought on the fields and on the mountains, on the grain, new wine, and oil, and on whatever the ground yields, on man and beast, and on all the labor of your hands.”

Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, as well as all the {{remnant|remnant}} of the people, obeyed the voice of the LORD their God and the words of the prophet Haggai, because the LORD their God had sent him. So the people feared the LORD.
Haggai, the messenger of the LORD, delivered the message of the LORD to the people: “{{immanu|I am with you}},” declares the LORD.

So the LORD {{stir|stirred}} the spirit of Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and the spirit of Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, as well as the spirit of all the remnant of the people. And they came and began the work on the house of the LORD of Hosts, their God,
on the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month, in the second year of King Darius.

lordOfHosts.gloss: The LORD of Hosts, or LORD of Armies. Tseva'ot means armies or hosts, the heavenly ranks and all the powers of creation. The title, which drums through Haggai fourteen times, presents the covenant God as the sovereign commander of every force in heaven and earth, more than able to fulfil what He promises to a small and struggling people.

ruins.gloss: Waste, desolate, laid in ruins. The same root can describe a dried up land or a devastated building. Haggai sets the ruined house of God against the people's finished, paneled houses, exposing where their true treasure lay.

consider.gloss: Literally, set your heart upon your ways. It is more than a passing thought; it is a summons to weigh life honestly before God, to trace the connection between spiritual neglect and empty results. Haggai repeats the call so it cannot be ignored.

glorified.gloss: To be heavy, weighty, honored, glorified. The root behind glory, kavod, carries the idea of weight and worth. God calls His people to build so that He may take pleasure in the house and display His weighty glory, the true purpose behind the timber and stone.

remnant.gloss: A remnant, remainder, those who are left. It names the small company that survived exile and returned to the land. The prophets often speak of a remnant preserved by grace through whom God keeps His promises alive.

immanu.gloss: I am with you. The great covenant assurance spoken to Isaac, Jacob, Moses, and Joshua, and gathered up in the name Immanuel, God with us. Here it turns fearful, discouraged builders into a working people, for the presence of God is their strength.

stir.gloss: To rouse, awaken, stir up. The LORD stirs the spirit of leaders and people alike, so their obedience is not mere willpower but the fruit of God awakening them. Human response and divine grace meet in the same moment.

basic.context: About eighteen years after the first exiles came home from Babylon, the temple of God still lay in ruins. The people had grown comfortable, finishing their own paneled houses while God's house sat as a weedy foundation. In 520 BC God raises up the prophet Haggai, who tells them plainly why nothing is going right: they have put themselves first and God's house last.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, on a datable day in history, and it teaches a lasting lesson about priorities. It is not a rule that every believer must build a physical building; it is a call to put God and His purposes first, and a warning that a life built around our own comfort leaves us strangely empty. Haggai twice says, consider your ways, and that call still lands on us.
basic.originalLanguages: God is called the LORD of Hosts, the commander of every army in heaven and earth, so His call carries real weight. He tells them to consider their ways, literally to set their hearts on their ways, to weigh their lives honestly. He wants the house built so He may be glorified, a word that means weighty, honored. And He stirs their spirit, awakening them to obey.
basic.typology: The temple was the place where God dwelt among His people. It points forward to Jesus, who is God dwelling with us, and to the church as God's living temple. The call to build God's house foreshadows the greater building work God does in Christ, gathering a people as His dwelling by the Spirit.
basic.christ: The whole point of the temple was that God would dwell with His people and be glorified. Jesus is the true temple, God present in the flesh, and He said something greater than the temple is here. The rebuilding of a humble house in Haggai's day was one small step in the long story that leads to Christ, in whom God fully dwells and through whom we are made His temple.
basic.god: God cares about His glory and about being first in the lives of His people, not out of vanity but because we were made for Him. He disciplines with hard providences to wake us up, yet the moment His people turn, He speaks the tender words, I am with you, and stirs their hearts to obey. He is both the God who confronts and the God who comes near.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus says to seek first the kingdom of God, and the rest will follow, Matthew 6:33. Paul calls the church the temple of God, 1 Corinthians 3:16. And the Lord promises never to leave nor forsake His people, Hebrews 13:5, the same assurance Haggai brings.
basic.oneStory: Haggai's call to rebuild is one thread in the Bible's long story of God dwelling with His people, from the garden, to the tabernacle, to the temple, to Christ, to the church, and finally to the new creation where God dwells with us forever. Rebuilding this modest house kept that story moving toward its fulfilment in Jesus.

context.literary: The first of Haggai's four precisely dated oracles, and the only one that also records the people's response at length. It moves from divine rebuke, through the twice repeated summons consider your ways, to the report of obedience, framed at both ends by the date and the names of Zerubbabel and Joshua. The prophet's terse, hard hitting style presses the people from complacency to action within a single chapter.
context.historical: Cyrus of Persia had allowed the exiles to return around 538 BC and to begin rebuilding the temple, but opposition, chiefly from the Samaritans described in Ezra 4, together with discouragement and hard times, stalled the work for about sixteen years. By the second year of Darius, 520 BC, the people had settled into their own homes while the temple foundation lay neglected. Haggai and his contemporary Zechariah were sent to revive the work, recorded in Ezra 5:1-2.
context.canonical: Haggai stands among the post-exilic prophets, third from the end of the Old Testament, alongside Zechariah and Malachi. It picks up the temple theme that runs from Exodus through Kings and forward into the Gospels, where Jesus is revealed as the true temple, and into the New Testament vision of the church and the new creation as God's dwelling.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront the returned community's spiritual apathy, to show that their frustrating, fruitless lives were tied to their neglect of God's house and God's glory, and to move them, leaders and people together, to obedient action in reliance on the promise of God's presence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific summons to rebuild a specific temple in 520 BC; it does not prescribe a building program for every generation. The abiding truths are that God rightly claims first place, that self-centred living leaves us empty, that His word calls for honest self-examination, and that His presence empowers obedience.

debates.question: Were the people's hard times a direct divine judgment or ordinary misfortune?

views.label: Direct covenant discipline
views.case: Haggai says plainly that God withheld dew and summoned a drought because His house lay in ruins, echoing the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 for a people who neglect the Lord.

views.label: Ordinary hardship interpreted by the prophet
views.case: The failed harvests could be read as the normal difficulties of a struggling post-exilic economy, which Haggai interprets prophetically to expose their spiritual root.

debates.bearing: Either way the message stands: God was using their circumstances to call them back to Himself, and the cure was to consider their ways and put His house first.

debates.question: How does I am with you relate to their obedience?

views.label: Assurance following obedience
views.case: The word comes after they obey and fear the LORD, sealing their renewed commitment with the promise of His presence.

views.label: Grace that enables obedience
views.case: The LORD then stirs their spirit to do the work, showing that even their willingness to build flows from His awakening grace, not from their resolve alone.

debates.bearing: The two hold together: God graciously stirs His people, and His abiding presence carries them through the work He has called them to do.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle turns on a few weighty words. God is the LORD of Hosts, YHWH tseva'ot, commander of the armies of heaven and earth, so His summons is no small thing. Twice He says sim lev, set your heart on your ways, a call to honest reckoning rather than a passing thought. The house lies chareb, waste and desolate, in pointed contrast to the people's finished homes. God wants it built so He may be kabad, glorified, weighty in honor, the very purpose of the temple. And when they respond, it is because the LORD ur, stirred, awakened, their spirit, so that grace and obedience meet in one act.

typology.type: The temple as God's dwelling place
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the Word made flesh, in whom God dwells among us, John 1:14 and 2:19-21, the true and greater temple.

typology.type: The rebuilt house of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The church, built together as a holy temple and a dwelling for God by the Spirit, Ephesians 2:19-22 and 1 Peter 2:5.
Immanuel, God with us in Christ, who promises His presence to the end of the age, Matthew 1:23 and 28:20.

study.christ: The temple existed so that God might dwell with His people and be glorified among them, and that longing finds its answer in Jesus. He is the true temple, the place where God and humanity meet, who spoke of the temple of His body raised in three days, John 2:19-21. He declared that something greater than the temple is here, Matthew 12:6. The modest house rebuilt in Haggai's day was a small but vital link in the chain that leads to Christ, and through Him to a people who are themselves being built into God's dwelling. The promise I am with you is gathered up in His name Immanuel and in His parting words, I am with you always.
study.god: The LORD of Hosts is jealous for His own glory and for first place in the hearts of His people, because that is what they were made for. He is willing to send lean and frustrating providences to wake a drowsy people, yet He is quick to comfort, speaking I am with you the moment they turn. He is sovereign, stirring the spirits of governor, priest, and people so that His work goes forward. He confronts and He draws near, and both are mercy.

crossReferences.note: Records the historical fruit of Haggai and Zechariah's preaching: Zerubbabel and Joshua resume building the house of God.
Seek first the kingdom of God, and all these things will be added, the positive form of Haggai's rebuke of misplaced priorities.
The church is now the temple of God, giving the temple theme its New Testament shape.
I will never leave you nor forsake you, the enduring promise behind Haggai's I am with you.

study.oneStory: Haggai's call to rebuild belongs to the Bible's great storyline of God making a home with His people, from Eden, to the tabernacle, to Solomon's temple, to this humble second temple, and on to Christ, in whom God dwells bodily, and to the church indwelt by His Spirit. Rebuilding the house kept the flame of that promise burning until the day the true temple came, and it points ahead to the new creation where God will dwell with His people forever.

sermon.title: Build God's House (Haggai Part 1)
sermon.why: From Legge's expository series Majoring On The Minors: Haggai, this message works through the whole of Haggai 1:1-15, unfolding the rebuke for procrastination, the reaping of poverty, and the renewal of purpose, with clear application to putting God first. A sound, orthodox exposition of this exact passage.

quiz.q: Why were the people's efforts leaving them empty and frustrated?

options.text: They had put their own comfort first and left God's house in ruins.
options.note: Haggai ties their fruitless lives directly to neglecting God's house and His glory.

options.text: They were simply going through a run of bad weather and poor harvests.
options.note: The hard times were real, but Haggai reveals their spiritual root: misplaced priorities.

options.text: They had not worked hard enough on their farms.
options.note: The problem was not lack of effort but a heart that put self before God.

options.text: God had simply abandoned them for no reason.
options.note: God had not abandoned them; He was calling them back and promising, I am with you.

quiz.q: What does the repeated call consider your ways mean?

options.text: Set your heart honestly on your life before God and trace the connection between neglect and emptiness.
options.note: The Hebrew sim lev means to weigh life seriously, not a passing thought.

options.text: Feel a little guilty and then carry on as before.
options.note: It calls for real reckoning that leads to change, not mere fleeting guilt.

options.text: Make a better financial plan for the year.
options.note: It is a spiritual summons about priorities before God, not budgeting advice.

options.text: Ignore the past and just think positively.
options.note: It is precisely a call to examine the past honestly before God.

quiz.q: Why did God want the temple rebuilt?

options.text: So that He might take pleasure in it and be glorified among His people.
options.note: Verse 8 gives the reason: that I may take pleasure in it and be glorified.

options.text: Mainly to give the people a big construction project to keep busy.
options.note: The work mattered, but its purpose was God's pleasure and glory, not busyness.

options.text: To make the nation wealthy and powerful again.
options.note: The aim was God's glory and presence, not national prosperity for its own sake.

options.text: Because God needed a building to live in.
options.note: God is not contained by buildings; the temple displayed His presence and glory.

quiz.q: What finally moved the people to obey and build?

options.text: The LORD stirred their spirit after the promise, I am with you.
options.note: Verses 13-14 show God's presence and His stirring grace behind their obedience.

options.text: They felt too ashamed not to do something.
options.note: Conviction played a part, but the text credits God stirring their spirit.

options.text: The governor forced them to work.
options.note: Zerubbabel led, but it was the LORD who stirred leaders and people alike.

options.text: The weather suddenly improved.
options.note: The turning point was God's word and His stirring, not a change in weather.

quiz.q: How does the temple in Haggai point to Christ?

options.text: It was the place of God's dwelling and glory, fulfilled in Jesus, the true temple, God with us.
options.note: Jesus is the greater temple where God dwells among us, John 2:19-21; Matthew 12:6.

options.text: It shows that God only ever wanted grand buildings.
options.note: The building served God's presence; that presence comes fully in Christ, not in architecture.

options.text: It proves believers must build literal temples today.
options.note: The church is now God's temple; the call is to God's dwelling, not a building program.

options.text: It has no connection to Jesus at all.
options.note: The whole temple theme leads to Christ, in whom God dwells bodily.

questions: The people finished their own paneled houses while God's house lay in ruins. Where might your own comfort be quietly crowding out God's rightful first place?
Twice God says, consider your ways. If you set your heart honestly on your life right now, where do you see emptiness that a reordering of priorities might address?
God tied their frustration to their neglect of Him. Are there frustrations in your life that might be an invitation to return to Him rather than merely a problem to fix?
Only four weeks passed between Haggai's word and the start of the work. Is there some obedience you have been putting off with the excuse that the time has not yet come?
God's tender word, I am with you, turned discouraged people into willing builders. How would truly believing that God is with you change the work in front of you this week?

translationNotes.note: The LORD of Hosts renders YHWH tseva'ot, the LORD of Armies, a title used repeatedly in Haggai to stress God's sovereign power over all heaven and earth.
Consider carefully your ways renders the Hebrew idiom set your heart upon your ways, a call to serious self-examination before God.
A bag pierced through, or a bag with holes, vividly pictures wages that slip away and never satisfy, whether through scarcity or inflation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Haggai 1:1-15. All 15 verses reproduced in order. The footnote noting Jehozadak as a variant of Jozadak (Ezra 3:2) is retained in the main text as printed by BSB.
